one.zh: 中期养育:父亲承认家暴、视频显示对四岁幼子严重过当管教,法院维持监督接触并责令制定针对性治疗计划,拒绝放宽为无监督。
one.en: Interim parenting: with the father admitting family violence and a video showing seriously excessive discipline of his four-year-old, the court kept the time supervised and ordered a targeted treatment plan, refusing unsupervised contact.

issue.zh: 在父亲承认家暴、且有证据显示其对四岁幼子严重过当管教的情况下,中期阶段应否将监督接触放宽为无监督?需要什么才能放宽?
issue.en: With the father admitting family violence and evidence of seriously excessive discipline of his four-year-old, should supervised time be relaxed to unsupervised at the interim stage — and what would it take?

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Interim parenting arrangements – Where there are two children to the marriage – Where the father seeks unsupervised time with one of the children and for family therapy to commence with the other – Orders made for the continuation of supervised time and for the father to undergo psychological treatment.

subseq.zh: 本判决为中期命令,非最终结论;监督接触、治疗计划等均为「待进一步命令」的临时安排,后续可随证据变化调整。父亲另涉刑事程序。
subseq.en: An interim decision, not a final outcome; the supervised time and treatment plan are 'until further order' arrangements, adjustable as evidence changes. The father also faces criminal proceedings.

t.zh: 故事 · 全本（涵盖全案）

hint.en: The whole case as a story

html.zh:
一个横跨印度、英国、澳大利亚的家庭。父亲 Mr Aliye(1985 年生)与母亲 Ms Salomea(1989 年生)育有两名年幼子女——X(2020 年生)与 Y(2022 年生)。

2025 年 3 月 27 日,父亲提起诉讼,要求母亲将孩子送回澳大利亚。背景是家庭冲突不断升级:父亲从印度前往英国(他称本打算再返回澳大利亚),在英国卷入法律程序并最终被捕。孩子们回到澳大利亚后,法院已作出命令——子女与母亲同住,其中一名子女 Y 与父亲进行监督接触(supervised time)。

到了本次中期(interim)聆讯,父亲提出两点请求:把监督接触改为无监督;并与他目前没有接触的另一名子女 X 开始家庭治疗。

但母亲 2026 年 1 月 23 日的最新誓章,披露了更细致的家庭暴力——尤其针对当时年仅四岁的儿子。一段视频显示父亲行为失控、管教明显过当:曾愤怒地用力击打孩子,并多次施以严重的言语辱骂。父亲承认自己曾对母亲和孩子施加家庭暴力。McNab 法官因此拒绝放宽接触,转而要求父亲先拿出切实正视问题的证据。
html.en:
A family spanning India, the United Kingdom and Australia. The father, Mr Aliye (born 1985), and the mother, Ms Salomea (born 1989), have two young children — X (born 2020) and Y (born 2022).

On 27 March 2025 the father began proceedings seeking the children's return to Australia. The backdrop was escalating conflict: the father had travelled from India to the UK (on his case, intending to return to Australia), became caught up in legal proceedings there, and was eventually arrested. After the children returned to Australia, orders were already in place — the children to live with the mother, with one child, Y, having supervised time with the father.

At this interim hearing the father asked for two things: that the supervised time become unsupervised, and that family therapy begin with the other child, X, whom he is not currently seeing.

But the mother's most recent affidavit of 23 January 2026 set out family violence in more detail — particularly toward the son, then only four. A video showed the father out of control and clearly excessive in his discipline: striking the child hard in anger, and subjecting him to severe verbal abuse on several occasions. The father accepted he had subjected the mother and children to family violence. McNab J therefore declined to relax the arrangements, instead requiring the father first to show real steps to address the problem.

html.zh:
性质:中期(interim)养育命令,非最终裁定。父亲另面临刑事指控,故法官刻意将理由限于必要范围,以免影响刑事程序。

时间线:2025-03-27 父亲提起诉讼(要求孩子返澳)→ 父亲在英国被捕的背景 → 孩子返澳,既有命令:与母同住、Y 与父监督接触 → 2025-09-16 既有命令(其中第 5–7 项本次予以保留)→ 2026-01-23 母亲最新誓章披露家暴细节 → 2026-01-29 本次中期聆讯及命令(Melbourne,McNab 法官)。
html.en:
Nature: an interim parenting decision, not a final determination. The father also faces criminal charges, so the judge deliberately kept the reasons to what was necessary, to avoid prejudicing the criminal proceedings.

Timeline: 27 Mar 2025 father commences proceedings (return of children) → backdrop of his arrest in the UK → children return to Australia; existing orders for them to live with the mother and Y to have supervised time → 16 Sep 2025 earlier orders (orders 5–7 preserved here) → 23 Jan 2026 mother's latest affidavit detailing family violence → 29 Jan 2026 this interim hearing and orders (Melbourne, McNab J).

html.zh:
§ s60CA ›
§ s60CC(2) ›
§ s60B ›
§ s64B ›
§ s65D(1) ›
框架
养育令依《1975 年家庭法》Part VII 作出:s 60CA 定下「子女最佳利益为首要考量」的总纲;s 60CC(2) 列出判断最佳利益须考量的事项(安全居核心);s 64B 界定何为「养育令」;s 65D(1) 授权法院作出其认为适当的养育令;s 60B 列明立法目的(保障子女最佳利益与安全,落实《儿童权利公约》)。
html.en:
§ s60CA ›
§ s60CC(2) ›
§ s60B ›
§ s64B ›
§ s65D(1) ›
Framework
Parenting orders are made under Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975: s 60CA sets the overarching rule that the child's best interests are paramount; s 60CC(2) lists the matters relevant to best interests (with safety central); s 64B defines a "parenting order"; s 65D(1) empowers the court to make such parenting orders as it thinks proper; s 60B states the objects (children's best interests and safety; giving effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child).

html.zh: 本案为中期判决,未援引判例。法官径直适用《1975 年家庭法》的成文框架——最佳利益为首要考量(s 60CA)、s 60CC(2) 因素清单、以及 Part VII 的立法目的(s 60B)——而非借助先例展开论证。
html.en: This interim decision cites no case law. The judge applied the statutory framework of the Family Law Act 1975 directly — best interests as the paramount consideration (s 60CA), the s 60CC(2) list of factors, and the objects of Part VII (s 60B) — rather than reasoning through precedent.

html.zh:
法官的推理简洁而清楚:

1. 首要标准 —— 作出任何养育令,子女最佳利益是首要(paramount)考量(s 60CA);s 60CC(2) 列出须考量的事项,其中安全居于核心。

2. 关键证据 —— 母亲 1 月 23 日誓章及一段视频显示,父亲对四岁儿子的管教严重过当:愤怒地用力击打、持续严重言语辱骂,且非一次而是多次。父亲本人承认曾对母亲与子女施加家庭暴力。

3. 风险未消除 —— 父亲虽在看心理医生,但现有有限证据看不出他在针对性地处理母亲证据所揭示的问题(家暴、对子女的管教、愤怒管理)。

4. 结论 —— 在父亲拿出「有方向、有力度地正视问题」的证据之前,法院不会把监督接触放宽为无监督;故命令其治疗心理医生制定一份针对家暴 / 管教 / 愤怒管理的治疗计划,并维持监督接触。

法官特别说明:鉴于父亲面临刑事指控,仅就判决所必需者陈述理由,以免影响刑事程序。
html.en:
The reasoning is short and clear:

1. The governing standard — in making any parenting order the child's best interests are the paramount consideration (s 60CA); s 60CC(2) lists the matters to be weighed, with safety central.

2. The key evidence — the mother's 23 January affidavit and a video showed the father's discipline of his four-year-old son was seriously excessive: striking the child hard in anger and sustained severe verbal abuse, on several occasions, not just once. The father himself accepted he had subjected the mother and children to family violence.

3. Risk not yet addressed — although the father was seeing a psychologist, the limited evidence gave no indication he was specifically addressing the issues the mother's evidence raised (family violence, his discipline of the children, anger management).

4. Conclusion — until the father produces evidence of a directed and concerted approach to those issues, the court will not move from supervised to unsupervised time; it therefore ordered his treating psychologist to prepare a treatment plan addressing family violence, discipline and anger management, and kept the time supervised.

The judge expressly noted that, with the father facing criminal charges, he would say no more than necessary so as not to prejudice those proceedings.

html.zh:
裁判要旨

在涉及家庭暴力的中期养育争议中,只要安全风险尚未通过针对性、可验证的干预得到处理,法院不会仅因家长正在接受一般性心理咨询,就将监督接触放宽为无监督接触。

子女安全作为最佳利益的核心,优先于家长扩大接触的请求;实际举证负担落在寻求放宽接触的一方,须证明其已切实正视并处理风险。
html.en:
Ratio

In an interim parenting dispute involving family violence, the court will not move from supervised to unsupervised time merely because a parent is engaged in general psychological counselling, so long as the safety risk has not been addressed by a targeted, verifiable intervention.

The child's safety, as the core of best interests, takes priority over a parent's request to expand contact; the practical onus falls on the parent seeking to relax the arrangements to show the risk has genuinely been confronted and addressed.

html.zh:
Interim parenting order · 中期养育令
最终裁定前的临时养育安排,可随证据变化调整(本案多项为「待进一步命令」)。
Supervised time · 监督接触
子女与家长相处时须有第三方(如接触中心)在场监督;本案由 D 接触中心提供,费用由父亲承担。
Best interests (paramount) · 子女最佳利益(首要)
判定一切子女事项的首要标准,高于其他考量(s 60CA)。
s 60CC(2) factors · 最佳利益因素清单
法院判断子女最佳利益须考量的法定事项,安全居核心。
Family violence · 家庭暴力
家庭成员间的暴力、威胁或胁迫行为;本案父亲已承认。
Parenting order · 养育令(s 64B)
关于子女居住、相处时间、亲职责任等事项的命令。
Hair follicle test · 毛囊检测
通过毛发样本检测一段时期内的物质(本案为酒精)使用情况。
html.en:
Interim parenting order
A temporary parenting arrangement pending final determination, adjustable as evidence changes (several orders here are 'until further order').
Supervised time
Time between child and parent that must occur with a third party (e.g. a contact centre) present; here via D Contact Centre, at the father's sole expense.
Best interests (paramount)
The overriding standard for all matters concerning a child, above other considerations (s 60CA).
s 60CC(2) factors
The statutory matters the court must weigh in assessing a child's best interests, with safety central.
Family violence
Violent, threatening or coercive conduct between family members; admitted by the father here.
Parenting order (s 64B)
An order about a child's living arrangements, time, parental responsibility and related matters.
Hair follicle test
Testing a hair sample to detect substance use (here, alcohol) over a period.

html.zh:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

MCNAB J

This matter concerns the applicant father, Mr Aliye born in 1985 (“the father”) and the respondent mother, Ms Salomea born in 1989 (“the mother”) in regards to the interim parenting arrangements for the children, X born in 2020 and Y born in 2022 (“the children”).

BACKGROUND

The father commenced these proceedings on 27 March 2025 seeking orders that the mother return the children to Australia. This was within the context of an escalation in conflict between the parties where the husband travelled to the United Kingdom from India, on his case, intending to return to Australia. There were legal proceedings in the United Kingdom and the father was eventually arrested. The children returned to Australia and orders were made for them to live with mother and for one of the children to spend supervised time with the father. The father now seeks, at this interim stage, that such time becomes unsupervised and family therapy commences with the child who he is not spending time with.

RELEVANT LAW

In deciding whether to make a particular parenting order in relation to the children, I must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.[1]

Section 60CC(2) of the Act sets out the list of matters that the Court must consider in determining what is in the children's best interests.

Orders in respect of children are made under Part VII of the Act, where the meaning of a “parenting order” is defined (s 64B). The Court may make such parenting orders as it thinks proper (s 65D(1)), within the context of the objects of the legislation (s 60B). The objects of Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (“the Act”) are to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety, and to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child of 20 November 1989.[2] My decision is guided by those objects.

CONSIDERATION

Having regard to all of the circumstances, I find it appropriate that supervised time continues and the father is to obtain directed psychological treatment and provide evidence of such. I find this for several reasons.

In her most recent affidavit of 23 January 2026, the mother provides a more detailed account of the family violence that occurred in the household, and more specifically, going to the father's conduct towards his son, who was at that time four years of age. A video of the father and the child shows that the father's behaviour was uncontrolled and showed clearly excessive discipline of the child. He raised his voice, he hit the child quite strongly in an angry forceful way and subjected the child to ongoing verbal abuse of a severe kind. As described in the affidavit, it was not on one occasion, but on several occasions, and the treatment of the child was totally inappropriate.

The father has accepted that he has subjected the mother and the children to family violence, but my concern is that the limited evidence before the Court as to his engagement with a psychologist does not give any indication that he is seeking to address the particular issues that arise as a result of the evidence of the mother.

The Court is not prepared to make an order for unsupervised time until the father has provided evidence to the Court of a directed and concerted approach to deal with the issues which arise from the evidence of the mother, and, for those reasons, the Court makes orders for his treating psychologist to provide a detailed treatment plan that addresses the issues of family violence, his discipline of the children and his anger management. The father is facing criminal charges, and I will not go beyond what is necessary to provide reasons for the judgment so as not to prejudice any criminal proceedings that he faces.

ORDERS (extract)

Within 7 days of these orders, the Father must book an appointment for the earliest supervised hair follicle alcohol test (“the hair follicle testing”).

Orders 5-7 of orders of 16 September 2025 remain of full force and effect.

The father continue to attend upon his treating psychologist, Dr B and provide a copy of the Psychological and Risk Assessment and Family Report of Dr C, a copy of the mother’s affidavit of 23 January 2026 (without annexures) and a copy of the Reasons of these orders to Dr B.

The father’s psychologist forthwith prepare a treatment plan for the father that directly addresses the father’s disciplining of his children and issues of family violence and anger management, and the father is to provide a copy of that plan to the mother’s legal representative within 7 days of receipt of same.

Supervised time

Until further order, the child Y spend supervised time with the father on one occasion of up to two hours per week on such days and at such times as can be accommodated by D Contact Centre (“Family Contact Service”).

Y’s time with the father be supervised by the Family Contact Service (or such other agreed supervisor) at the father’s sole expense.

In relation to the parties’ engagement with the Family Contact Service:

(a) The parties do all acts and things and provide all documents required by the Family Contact Service for the purposes of intake as stated in paragraph 4 of these Orders;

(b) The parties follow all lawful directions of the Family Contact Service; and

(c) Supervised sessions shall cease or be shortened immediately if Y becomes distressed or upset, as assessed by the supervising staff.

The father shall be responsible for payment of the costs associated with obtaining an Observation Report after 8 sessions from the Family Contact Service, with such Report to be released directly to the parties’ legal representatives.

The father shall file a copy of the report from the Family Contact Service by way of affidavit upon the Observation Report being available.

Supervised time at the Family Contact Service may continue only upon the completion of a positive Observational Report and shall continue until such time as further orders are made in relation to ongoing time.

Order 11 of the Orders of 16 September 2015 be discharged.

The mother authorise X’s school to permit the father to receive routine school communications (including newsletters, notices and reports) and access student information for X via the school portal and/or usual school communication channels only, in accordance with the school’s policies and procedures.

The parties are at liberty to provide a copy of the Court orders to X’s School under Order 12.

The parties be at liberty to provide to X’s psychologist, Ms E and X’s paediatrician, Mr F a copy of the family report and psychological risk assessment ofDr C.

Pursuant to s 67ZBD of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), the Department of Families, Fairness and Housing (“the Department”) is to inform the Court by no later than 4.00 pm on2 March 2026 if it has in its possession or control any documents or information, and if so the particulars of the documents or information relating to the matters in s 67ZBD(2) in relation to:

(a) X born in 2020 (Child)

(b) Y born in 2022 (Child)

(c) Mr Aliye born in 1985 (Father of the children)

(d) Ms Salomea born in 1989 (Mother of the children)

The parties be restrained from issuing any subsequent subpoena to the Department seeking the production of any information or documents identified pursuant to these orders, except with the leave of the Court.

The matter be adjourned before the Hon. Justice McNab on 18 May 2026 at 10.00am for interim defended hearing to be conducted in person.

AND THE COURT NOTES THAT:

The particulars to be provided by the Department pursuant to Order 15 above may relate to documents or information relating to the following matters set out in s 67ZBD(2):

(a) abuse, neglect or family violence to which a child to whom the proceedings relate has been, or is suspected to have been, subjected or exposed;

(b) family violence to which a party to the proceedings has been exposed, or in which a party to the proceedings has engaged, to the extent it may affect a child to whom the proceedings relate;

(c) any risk or potential risk of a child to whom the proceedings relate being subjected or exposed to abuse, neglect or family violence; and

(d) any risk or potential risk of a party to the proceedings being subjected to, or engaging in, family violence, to the extent any such family violence may affect a child to whom the proceedings relate.

Pursuant to Division 1B of Part XI of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), that an application may be made for a direction that a document or part of a document not be produced or not be inspected or not be copied in proceedings if it would disclose a protected confidence.

Material provided by the Department may be redacted pursuant to s 67ZBF.

Pursuant to s 67ZBH (1), the Court must admit into evidence any particulars, documents or information provided under an Order made under s 67ZBD, or under s 67ZBD(5), on which the Court intends to rely.

Note: The form of the order is subject to the entry in the Court’s records.

Note: This copy of the Court’s Reasons for judgment may be subject to review to remedy minor typographical or grammatical errors (r 10.14(b) Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth)), or to record a variation to the order pursuant to r 10.13 Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth).

Part XIVB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) makes it an offence, except in very limited circumstances, to publish an account of proceedings that identify persons, associated persons, or witnesses involved in family law proceedings.

IT IS NOTED that publication of this judgment by this Court under the pseudonym Aliye & Salomea has been approved pursuant to subsection 114Q(2) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth).

EX TEMPORE
html.en:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

MCNAB J

This matter concerns the applicant father, Mr Aliye born in 1985 (“the father”) and the respondent mother, Ms Salomea born in 1989 (“the mother”) in regards to the interim parenting arrangements for the children, X born in 2020 and Y born in 2022 (“the children”).

BACKGROUND

The father commenced these proceedings on 27 March 2025 seeking orders that the mother return the children to Australia. This was within the context of an escalation in conflict between the parties where the husband travelled to the United Kingdom from India, on his case, intending to return to Australia. There were legal proceedings in the United Kingdom and the father was eventually arrested. The children returned to Australia and orders were made for them to live with mother and for one of the children to spend supervised time with the father. The father now seeks, at this interim stage, that such time becomes unsupervised and family therapy commences with the child who he is not spending time with.

RELEVANT LAW

In deciding whether to make a particular parenting order in relation to the children, I must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.[1]

Section 60CC(2) of the Act sets out the list of matters that the Court must consider in determining what is in the children's best interests.

Orders in respect of children are made under Part VII of the Act, where the meaning of a “parenting order” is defined (s 64B). The Court may make such parenting orders as it thinks proper (s 65D(1)), within the context of the objects of the legislation (s 60B). The objects of Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (“the Act”) are to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety, and to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child of 20 November 1989.[2] My decision is guided by those objects.

CONSIDERATION

Having regard to all of the circumstances, I find it appropriate that supervised time continues and the father is to obtain directed psychological treatment and provide evidence of such. I find this for several reasons.

In her most recent affidavit of 23 January 2026, the mother provides a more detailed account of the family violence that occurred in the household, and more specifically, going to the father's conduct towards his son, who was at that time four years of age. A video of the father and the child shows that the father's behaviour was uncontrolled and showed clearly excessive discipline of the child. He raised his voice, he hit the child quite strongly in an angry forceful way and subjected the child to ongoing verbal abuse of a severe kind. As described in the affidavit, it was not on one occasion, but on several occasions, and the treatment of the child was totally inappropriate.

The father has accepted that he has subjected the mother and the children to family violence, but my concern is that the limited evidence before the Court as to his engagement with a psychologist does not give any indication that he is seeking to address the particular issues that arise as a result of the evidence of the mother.

The Court is not prepared to make an order for unsupervised time until the father has provided evidence to the Court of a directed and concerted approach to deal with the issues which arise from the evidence of the mother, and, for those reasons, the Court makes orders for his treating psychologist to provide a detailed treatment plan that addresses the issues of family violence, his discipline of the children and his anger management. The father is facing criminal charges, and I will not go beyond what is necessary to provide reasons for the judgment so as not to prejudice any criminal proceedings that he faces.

ORDERS (extract)

Within 7 days of these orders, the Father must book an appointment for the earliest supervised hair follicle alcohol test (“the hair follicle testing”).

Orders 5-7 of orders of 16 September 2025 remain of full force and effect.

The father continue to attend upon his treating psychologist, Dr B and provide a copy of the Psychological and Risk Assessment and Family Report of Dr C, a copy of the mother’s affidavit of 23 January 2026 (without annexures) and a copy of the Reasons of these orders to Dr B.

The father’s psychologist forthwith prepare a treatment plan for the father that directly addresses the father’s disciplining of his children and issues of family violence and anger management, and the father is to provide a copy of that plan to the mother’s legal representative within 7 days of receipt of same.

Supervised time

Until further order, the child Y spend supervised time with the father on one occasion of up to two hours per week on such days and at such times as can be accommodated by D Contact Centre (“Family Contact Service”).

Y’s time with the father be supervised by the Family Contact Service (or such other agreed supervisor) at the father’s sole expense.

In relation to the parties’ engagement with the Family Contact Service:

(a) The parties do all acts and things and provide all documents required by the Family Contact Service for the purposes of intake as stated in paragraph 4 of these Orders;

(b) The parties follow all lawful directions of the Family Contact Service; and

(c) Supervised sessions shall cease or be shortened immediately if Y becomes distressed or upset, as assessed by the supervising staff.

The father shall be responsible for payment of the costs associated with obtaining an Observation Report after 8 sessions from the Family Contact Service, with such Report to be released directly to the parties’ legal representatives.

The father shall file a copy of the report from the Family Contact Service by way of affidavit upon the Observation Report being available.

Supervised time at the Family Contact Service may continue only upon the completion of a positive Observational Report and shall continue until such time as further orders are made in relation to ongoing time.

Order 11 of the Orders of 16 September 2015 be discharged.

The mother authorise X’s school to permit the father to receive routine school communications (including newsletters, notices and reports) and access student information for X via the school portal and/or usual school communication channels only, in accordance with the school’s policies and procedures.

The parties are at liberty to provide a copy of the Court orders to X’s School under Order 12.

The parties be at liberty to provide to X’s psychologist, Ms E and X’s paediatrician, Mr F a copy of the family report and psychological risk assessment ofDr C.

Pursuant to s 67ZBD of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), the Department of Families, Fairness and Housing (“the Department”) is to inform the Court by no later than 4.00 pm on2 March 2026 if it has in its possession or control any documents or information, and if so the particulars of the documents or information relating to the matters in s 67ZBD(2) in relation to:

(a) X born in 2020 (Child)

(b) Y born in 2022 (Child)

(c) Mr Aliye born in 1985 (Father of the children)

(d) Ms Salomea born in 1989 (Mother of the children)

The parties be restrained from issuing any subsequent subpoena to the Department seeking the production of any information or documents identified pursuant to these orders, except with the leave of the Court.

The matter be adjourned before the Hon. Justice McNab on 18 May 2026 at 10.00am for interim defended hearing to be conducted in person.

AND THE COURT NOTES THAT:

The particulars to be provided by the Department pursuant to Order 15 above may relate to documents or information relating to the following matters set out in s 67ZBD(2):

(a) abuse, neglect or family violence to which a child to whom the proceedings relate has been, or is suspected to have been, subjected or exposed;

(b) family violence to which a party to the proceedings has been exposed, or in which a party to the proceedings has engaged, to the extent it may affect a child to whom the proceedings relate;

(c) any risk or potential risk of a child to whom the proceedings relate being subjected or exposed to abuse, neglect or family violence; and

(d) any risk or potential risk of a party to the proceedings being subjected to, or engaging in, family violence, to the extent any such family violence may affect a child to whom the proceedings relate.

Pursuant to Division 1B of Part XI of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), that an application may be made for a direction that a document or part of a document not be produced or not be inspected or not be copied in proceedings if it would disclose a protected confidence.

Material provided by the Department may be redacted pursuant to s 67ZBF.

Pursuant to s 67ZBH (1), the Court must admit into evidence any particulars, documents or information provided under an Order made under s 67ZBD, or under s 67ZBD(5), on which the Court intends to rely.

Note: The form of the order is subject to the entry in the Court’s records.

Note: This copy of the Court’s Reasons for judgment may be subject to review to remedy minor typographical or grammatical errors (r 10.14(b) Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth)), or to record a variation to the order pursuant to r 10.13 Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth).

Part XIVB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) makes it an offence, except in very limited circumstances, to publish an account of proceedings that identify persons, associated persons, or witnesses involved in family law proceedings.

IT IS NOTED that publication of this judgment by this Court under the pseudonym Aliye & Salomea has been approved pursuant to subsection 114Q(2) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth).

EX TEMPORE

desc.zh: 作出养育令时,须以子女最佳利益为首要考量——判定总纲。
desc.en: In deciding whether to make a parenting order, the child's best interests are the paramount consideration.

desc.zh: 列出法院判断子女最佳利益时须考量的事项,其中保障子女安全居于核心。
desc.en: Sets out the matters the court must consider in determining a child's best interests, with the child's safety central.

desc.zh: 列明 Part VII 的立法目的:保障子女最佳利益与安全,并落实《儿童权利公约》。
desc.en: States the objects of Part VII: to secure children's best interests and safety, and give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child.

desc.zh: 界定何为养育令——涵盖子女居住、相处时间、亲职责任等命令。
desc.en: Defines a 'parenting order' — covering a child's living arrangements, time, parental responsibility and related matters.

desc.zh: 授权法院作出其认为适当(as it thinks proper)的养育令。
desc.en: Empowers the court to make such parenting orders as it thinks proper.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

MCNAB J

This matter concerns the applicant father, Mr Aliye born in 1985 (“the father”) and the respondent mother, Ms Salomea born in 1989 (“the mother”) in regards to the interim parenting arrangements for the children, X born in 2020 and Y born in 2022 (“the children”).

BACKGROUND

The father commenced these proceedings on 27 March 2025 seeking orders that the mother return the children to Australia. This was within the context of an escalation in conflict between the parties where the husband travelled to the United Kingdom from India, on his case, intending to return to Australia. There were legal proceedings in the United Kingdom and the father was eventually arrested. The children returned to Australia and orders were made for them to live with mother and for one of the children to spend supervised time with the father. The father now seeks, at this interim stage, that such time becomes unsupervised and family therapy commences with the child who he is not spending time with.

RELEVANT LAW

In deciding whether to make a particular parenting order in relation to the children, I must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.[1]
Section 60CC(2) of the Act sets out the list of matters that the Court must consider in determining what is in the children's best interests.
Orders in respect of children are made under Part VII of the Act, where the meaning of a “parenting order” is defined (s 64B). The Court may make such parenting orders as it thinks proper (s 65D(1)), within the context of the objects of the legislation (s 60B). The objects of Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (“the Act”) are to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety, and to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child of 20 November 1989.[2] My decision is guided by those objects.

CONSIDERATION

Having regard to all of the circumstances, I find it appropriate that supervised time continues and the father is to obtain directed psychological treatment and provide evidence of such. I find this for several reasons.
In her most recent affidavit of 23 January 2026, the mother provides a more detailed account of the family violence that occurred in the household, and more specifically, going to the father's conduct towards his son, who was at that time four years of age. A video of the father and the child shows that the father's behaviour was uncontrolled and showed clearly excessive discipline of the child. He raised his voice, he hit the child quite strongly in an angry forceful way and subjected the child to ongoing verbal abuse of a severe kind. As described in the affidavit, it was not on one occasion, but on several occasions, and the treatment of the child was totally inappropriate.
The father has accepted that he has subjected the mother and the children to family violence, but my concern is that the limited evidence before the Court as to his engagement with a psychologist does not give any indication that he is seeking to address the particular issues that arise as a result of the evidence of the mother.
The Court is not prepared to make an order for unsupervised time until the father has provided evidence to the Court of a directed and concerted approach to deal with the issues which arise from the evidence of the mother, and, for those reasons, the Court makes orders for his treating psychologist to provide a detailed treatment plan that addresses the issues of family violence, his discipline of the children and his anger management. The father is facing criminal charges, and I will not go beyond what is necessary to provide reasons for the judgment so as not to prejudice any criminal proceedings that he faces.
